pattern: With only a few hundred operations total, there is no need for a fancy range-update structure — just overwrite the actual cells of the matrix on every update and read a cell directly on every query.
hints: Keep the matrix exactly as given, mutating it in place; nothing about the operation counts here demands anything smarter than touching real cells.
updateSubrectangle iterates every row from row1..row2 and every column from col1..col2 (inclusive on both ends) and overwrites each with newValue.
getValue is then just a single array lookup at [row][col] — no auxiliary bookkeeping needed since the matrix itself always reflects the latest state.

edge_inputs.description: single full-matrix update overwrites every cell
two overlapping updates: the later one wins on the overlap, the earlier one survives outside it
1x1 matrix: update and read the only cell
update a single-cell subrectangle inside a larger matrix leaves every neighbor untouched
a tall, narrow subrectangle (many rows, one column) must update the whole column span, not just the corners
three sequential overlapping updates on the same cell must leave only the most recent value

stress.description: random small matrix with a long sequence of random subrectangle updates and point queries
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    rows = rng.randint(1, 12)
    cols = rng.randint(1, 12)
    rect = [[rng.randint(1, 1000) for _ in range(cols)] for _ in range(rows)]
    ops = ['SubrectangleQueries']
    args = [[rect]]
    for _ in range(max(1, size)):
        if rng.random() < 0.5:
            r1 = rng.randint(0, rows - 1)
            r2 = rng.randint(r1, rows - 1)
            c1 = rng.randint(0, cols - 1)
            c2 = rng.randint(c1, cols - 1)
            ops.append('updateSubrectangle')
            args.append([r1, c1, r2, c2, rng.randint(1, 1000)])
        else:
            ops.append('getValue')
            args.append([rng.randint(0, rows - 1), rng.randint(0, cols - 1)])
    return [ops, args]
stress.note: row2/col2 are always sampled >= row1/col1 so every generated update is a valid (non-empty) subrectangle

complexity.time: O(rows * cols) per update, O(1) per query
